section.title: Introduction
section.para: After scanning your system, you can remediate identified policy violations automatically or manually. The &ssg; provides fix scripts in two formats — shell scripts and &ansible; playbooks — that oscap can apply directly, or that you can review and run independently.

important.title: Automatic remediation not always available
important.para: Automatic remediation is not offered for fixes that are too disruptive to apply safely on a running system. Such rules must be remediated manually.

section.para: The overall remediation process is as follows:

step.para: oscap scans the system and marks each failing rule as a candidate for remediation.
For each failing rule, oscap locates the corresponding xccdf:fix element in the XCCDF file, prepares the environment, and executes the fix script.
After executing the fix, oscap re-evaluates the rule to confirm whether the fix was successful.
All remediation results are stored in an output XCCDF file.

note.para: Because &slem; is an immutable system, remediation must be run more than once with reboots between passes. The first pass uses &tr-up; to apply changes to a new snapshot. After rebooting into the new snapshot, a second pass applies any remaining fixes. Rules are executed in alphabetical order and some have dependencies on others.

section.title: Remediating on the fly
section.para: The simplest approach is to scan and remediate in a single command using the --remediate option. The system is first scanned, and then oscap immediately attempts to fix each failing rule.

warning.title: Usage of the --skip-rule option
warning.para: Always use --skip-rule to skip the rule xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_accounts_authorized_local_users unless you have explicitly configured the variable var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex. Failing to do so may prevent sudo from working after a reboot.

step.para: Run the first remediation pass inside a transactional update:
Reboot the system to switch to the new snapshot:
Run the second remediation pass:
Reboot the system to apply the changes:

section.para: In the resulting results files, a rule result of fixed indicates a successful fix. A result of error indicates that the fix was not successful and the rule still does not pass evaluation.

section.title: Remediating after scanning
section.para: Alternatively, you can separate the scan and remediation into two steps. This allows you to review the scan results before applying any fixes.

step.para: Scan the system and save the results:
The results are stored in a TestResult element in /tmp/results.xml. The system is evaluated only — no changes are made at this stage.
Run the first remediation pass inside a transactional update:
Reboot the system to switch to the new snapshot:
Run a second remediation pass:
Reboot the system to apply the changes:

section.title: Generating remediation scripts for review
section.para: To inspect the remediation instructions before applying them, use oscap xccdf generate fix to save the fix content to a file without executing it.

section.title: Remediating with &ssg; shell scripts
section.para: The &ssg; ships a pre-built shell script for the general profile. This can be used for straightforward remediation without conditions or tailoring.

step.para: List the available shell scripts:
The script for &slem; follows the format slmicro6-script-PROFILE-NAME.sh. For the general profile, the script is slmicro6-script-general.sh.
Make the script executable:
Run the script inside a transactional update:
Reboot the system to switch to the new snapshot:
Run the script again:
Reboot the system to apply the changes:

note.para: Within each script, rules follow the format: # BEGIN fix (N/TOTAL) for RULE-ID through to # END fix for RULE-ID. Rules that contain a line ending with IS MISSING! have no automatic remediation and must be applied manually.

section.title: Remediating with &ansible; playbooks
section.para: The &ssg; also ships an &ansible; playbook for the general profile. The ansible package is available from &ph;. Because &slem; is an immutable system, install it using &tr-up;:

step.para: List the available &ansible; playbooks:
The playbook for &slem; follows the format slmicro6-playbook-PROFILE-NAME.yml. For the general profile, the playbook is slmicro6-playbook-general.yml.
Create an inventory file ansible_inventory.yml with the following content:
step.screen: all: hosts: localhost vars: ansible_connection: local

step.para: Run the playbook inside a transactional update:
Reboot the system to switch to the new snapshot:
Run the playbook again:
Reboot the system to apply the changes:
To skip specific rules during execution, use the --tags option. Find the tag for a rule by searching for it in the playbook file. For example:

note.para: You may need to repeat the remediation steps more than once. Some rules require a system restart to take effect, and rules are executed in alphabetical order, which means dependencies between rules may not be resolved in a single pass.

section.title: Applying a tailoring file
section.para: If you need to customize the general profile — for example, to exclude specific rules — create a tailoring file on a separate machine using SCAP Workbench, then transfer it to the &slem; system. Apply it with the --tailoring-file option:
